title: Uniaxial-Stress-Induced Magnetic Transitions in the Triangular-Lattice Antiferromagnet PdCrO2
authors: Nina Stilkerich, Tobias Ritschel, Hilary M. L. Noad, Richard Waite, Dmitry Khalyavin, Kousuke Ishida, Pascal Manuel, Fabio Orlandi, Seunghyun Khim, Elena Gati, Andrew P. Mackenzie, Jochen Geck, Clifford W. Hicks
abstract: Uniaxial stress is a promising method to tune magnetic frustration, allowing its effects to be studied in a precise way. In this work, uniaxial stress is applied to the triangular-lattice antiferromagnet PdCrO2. The Cr-Cr magnetic interaction is very sensitive to interatomic separation, so laboratory-achievable stress can induce substantial changes in magnetic structure. Results from three types of measurement are presented: X-ray diffraction, the stress-strain relationship, and neutron diffraction. The combined data show that the elastic moduli of PdCrO2 are strongly affected by stress-induced changes in magnetic structure. A new, first-order stress-induced magnetic transition is observed, at which the lattice constant shrinks by 0.21%. The lattice stiffens dramatically across this transition: the Young's modulus increases by about 80 GPa, and the Poisson ratio falls from about 1 to about 0.4. This stiffening indicates that the magnetic order "locks," that is, becomes insensitive to lattice strain. This locking might occur because the new stress-induced magnetic order nests the Fermi surface of the Pd sheets. Other frustrated magnets, including candidate spin liquids, may show similarly strong coupling between magnetic and elastic degrees of freedom.
summary: This work experimentally and theoretically investigates how uniaxial stress controls magnetic transitions in the triangular-lattice antiferromagnet PdCrO2. By combining XRD, mechanical testing, and neutron scattering, the authors reveal a first-order transition where the lattice stiffens dramatically, suggesting the magnetic order becomes rigid due to coupling with the Fermi surface. This highlights strong magnetoelastic coupling as a tunable mechanism for controlling quantum states in correlated materials.
main_problem: To investigate how applying uniaxial stress can tune magnetic frustration and induce first-order magnetic transitions in the triangular-lattice antiferromagnet PdCrO2.
main_result: The application of stress induces a new, first-order magnetic transition characterized by a dramatic stiffening (Young's modulus increase of ~80 GPa) of the lattice, suggesting the magnetic order 'locks' into a strain-insensitive state.
method: A combination of experimental techniques—X-ray diffraction, uniaxial stress-strain measurements, and neutron diffraction—is used alongside theoretical modeling to probe the magnetoelastic coupling.
model_or_system: The system is PdCrO2, a triangular-lattice antiferromagnet. The physics involves minimizing the total free energy, which couples the elastic energy (governed by lattice strain) with the magnetic interaction energy (governed by spin configuration).
key_observables: Elastic moduli (Young's modulus, Poisson ratio), lattice constants ($a, b$), and magnetic Bragg reflection intensities/positions under applied stress ($\sigma_{xx}$).
important_parameters: Uniaxial strain ($\varepsilon_{xx}$), temperature ($T=14 	ext{ K}, 45 	ext{ K}$), and the magnitude of the stress-induced lattice constant shrinkage (0.21%).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations like the 'two-spring model' for strain calculation, and theoretical models assume specific dependencies of magnetic coupling ($J$) on interatomic separation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate XRD data processing, stress vs. strain curves showing sharp jumps at transitions, and neutron scattering intensity cuts confirming phase changes and structural domain types.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the experimental necessity of studying magnetoelastic coupling via uniaxial stress. It then presents combined evidence from XRD (lattice parameters), $\sigma-\epsilon$ (elastic moduli), and neutron diffraction (magnetic order) to pinpoint a novel, rigid magnetic phase transition.
why_interesting: The strong, measurable coupling between mechanical strain and electronic/magnetic state is central to understanding emergent quantum phenomena in correlated materials. The concept of 'locking' due to Fermi surface nesting provides a concrete mechanism for such magnetoelastic rigidity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Lattice directions and hypothesised spin configura- tions under strain. (a) Schematic of a triangular lattice under uniaxial stress applied along the [210] lattice direction. J1 is the exchange energy across the bonds that get compressed (solid lines), and J2 across those that get stretched (dashed lines). The blue lines are high-symmetry lattice directions. The x, y, and z directions, used in discussion of strain, are also shown. (b) 120◦spin spiral, obtained when J2 = J1. The coloured lines are magnetic Bragg planes. (c) 150◦spin spiral, obtained for J2 = 0.5774J1 (neglecting all longer-range interactions). (d) N´eel order. (e) Motion of the magnetic reflections as the mag-...

Figure 2. Experimental information on XRD measurement under strain. (a) Setup for the X-ray diffraction measurement with detector angle ζ, cryostat rotation ω, sample rotation φ, and sample tilt χ labelled. (b) Unit cell of PdCrO2; this cell is of the obverse type. (c) A parametrisation of this cell. (d) Sum of area detector images over varying ω, focused on the 2¯2¯5 reflection. (e) Azimuthally integrated intensity of the 2¯2¯5 reflection (black line), along with a fit (blue dashed line).
Figure 5. Differential Young’s modulus and Poisson ratio. (a) Differential Young’s modulus E of sample B at 15 K, and differential Poisson ratio ν of sample A at 14 K. (b) Equivalent data at 45 K.

Figure 4. Stress-strain relationship and differential Young’s modulus. (a) Stress σxx versus strain εxx for sample B at various temperatures. (b) Differential Young’s modulus E = dσxx/dεxx for sample B at 5.0 K, with the strain ranges of the three different phases labelled. (c) Differential Young’s modulus of sample B at various temperatures. (d) Same for sample C. In all the panels, the calibration strain, −0.69 · 10−2, obtained from XRD data is shown by a vertical dashed line.

Figure 6. Phase diagram. (a) Stress-temperature phase diagram of PdCrO2, derived from stress-strain data on sample B. (b) Jump in strain ∆ε at the two first-order transitions, as determined from stress-strain data on sample B. Due to artefacts introduced by the two-spring approximation, these values for ∆ε are about 70% of the true strain jumps at the transitions.
